verses.text: After Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar; and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim. He judged Israel twenty and three years, and died, and was buried in Shamir. Guidance: After the bloody noise of Abimelech, two quiet judges. Tola — his name means 'worm' — gets two verses for twenty-three years. The opposite of Abimelech: he 'arose to defend,' he 'judged,' he 'died,' he 'was buried.' No drama. Sometimes the leaders who hold the line are the ones whose decades don't make headlines. The Spirit gives them the shortest paragraphs and the longest tenures.

After Abimelech there arose to defend Israel Tola son of Puah, the son of Dodo, a man of Issachar; and he dwelt in Shamir in mount Ephraim. He judged Israel twenty and three years, and died, and was buried in Shamir. Guidance: After the bloody noise of Abimelech, two quiet judges. Tola — his name means 'worm' — gets two verses for twenty-three years. The opposite of Abimelech: he 'arose to defend,' he 'judged,' he 'died,' he 'was buried.' No drama. Sometimes the leaders who hold the line are the ones whose decades don't make headlines. The Spirit gives them the shortest paragraphs and the longest tenures.

After him arose Jair, a Gileadite, and judged Israel twenty and two years. He had thirty sons that rode on thirty ass colts, and they had thirty cities, which are called Havoth-jair unto this day, which are in the land of Gilead. And Jair died, and was buried in Camon. Guidance: Thirty sons, thirty donkeys, thirty cities — the verse repeats the number three times. Jair is wealthy by tribal-chieftain standards. The sons riding donkeys hints at near-royal trappings without the title. The 'thirty cities' bear the family name 'Havvoth-jair' — Jair's villages — a rare case of a judge's clan leaving its mark on the map. Comfortable seasons are not necessarily wrong, but they tend to plant the seeds of the next decline. After Jair dies, Israel's idolatry will be its widest yet.

The children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD, and served Baalim, and Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, and the gods of Zidon, and the gods of Moab, and the gods of the children of Ammon, and the gods of the Philistines, and forsook the LORD, and served not him. The anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines, and into the hands of the children of Ammon. That year they vexed and oppressed the children of Israel: eighteen years, all the children of Israel that were on the other side Jordan in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gilead. Moreover the children of Ammon passed over Jordan to fight also against Judah, and against Benjamin, and against the house of Ephraim; so that Israel was sore distressed. Guidance: The seven-fold idolatry list is the chapter's grimmest sentence. Israel has gone past Baalim and Ashtaroth to importing the gods of every surrounding nation — Syria, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, Philistia. They run a religious bazaar. The LORD's response is twofold: a double oppression (Philistines on the west, Ammonites on the east). 'Sore distressed' — the same Hebrew phrase used of childbirth pains. The shape of God's discipline is exactly the shape of their disobedience: serve seven nations' gods, suffer at the hands of the same nations.

The children of Israel cried unto the LORD, saying, We have sinned against thee, both because we have forsaken our God, and also served Baalim. The LORD said unto the children of Israel, Did not I deliver you from the Egyptians, and from the Amorites, from the children of Ammon, and from the Philistines? The Zidonians also, and the Amalekites, and the Maonites, did oppress you; and ye cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand. Yet ye have forsaken me, and served other gods: wherefore I will deliver you no more. Go and cry unto the gods which ye have chosen; let them deliver you in the time of your tribulation. Guidance: An astonishing moment. Israel's confession is real ('we have sinned'). But God's reply is the harshest in Judges: 'go cry to the gods you have chosen.' Seven deliverances are listed; the verdict is exhaustion. Tough love is the right phrase. God is not changeable — His covenant remains — but He refuses to be treated as a fire department. He will not be the back-up plan for failed pagan worship. Sometimes the LORD's most loving move is to refuse to rescue too quickly. The verse forces a deeper repentance than the people first offered.

The children of Israel said unto the LORD, We have sinned: do thou unto us whatsoever seemeth good unto thee; deliver us only, we pray thee, this day. They put away the strange gods from among them, and served the LORD: and his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel. Guidance: This time the confession is different. No bargaining ('we'll do this if you do that'). They throw themselves on God: 'do whatsoever seemeth good.' And they do not stop at words. They 'put away the strange gods' — the actual idols in their homes. Confession backed by housecleaning. And then the chapter's tenderest phrase: 'his soul was grieved for the misery of Israel.' The God who refused them five verses ago is already grieving with them. He never stopped loving; He only stopped rescuing prematurely. Real repentance unlocks what shallow apology could not.

Then the children of Ammon were gathered together, and encamped in Gilead. And the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and encamped in Mizpeh. The people and princes of Gilead said one to another, What man is he that will begin to fight against the children of Ammon? he shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead. Guidance: Two armies, both encamped, both waiting. Israel has no general. The Gilead leaders make an open offer: 'whoever leads the charge becomes our chief.' It is a desperate bargain — they will give the office to whoever shows the courage first. The chapter ends on a cliffhanger. The text has just made the people repent and asked for a leader; the next chapter will introduce a man currently in exile, banished by his own brothers — Jephthah. Where the leaders' search committee has come up empty, God already has a candidate they had previously rejected.
